title: Ramanujan Conjecture and the Density Hypothesis
speaker: Shai Evra
abstract: The Generalized Ramanujan Conjecture (GRC) for GL(n) is a central open problem in modern number theory. Its resolution is known to yield several important applications. For instance, the Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture for GL(2), proven by Deligne, was a key ingredient in the work of Lubotzky-Phillips-Sarnak on Ramanujan graphs. One can also state analogues (Naive) Ramanujan Conjectures (NRC) for other reductive groups. However, in the 70's Kurokawa and Howe-Piatetski-Shapiro proved that the (NRC) fails even for quasi-split classical groups.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic-complex motivation and Hecke spectrum dictionary
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the problem by comparing arithmetic manifolds with p-adic arithmetic complexes, explaining that algebraic modular forms live on such finite complexes and that Hecke operators become adjacency operators whose spectra are controlled by Ramanujan-type bounds.

proof_stages.label: From classical Ramanujan to automorphic temperedness
proof_stages.summary: The speaker traces Ramanujan's tau conjecture through modular forms and Satake's reformulation, then defines automorphic representations, cuspidality, and temperedness to state the generalized Ramanujan conjecture and the known self-dual cohomological Ramanujan-Petersson theorem.

proof_stages.label: Density hypothesis as a replacement for false naive Ramanujan
proof_stages.summary: After showing that the naive Ramanujan conjecture fails for general reductive and even classical groups, the Sarnak-Xue density hypothesis is formulated using analytic conductor, decay of matrix coefficients, and a chosen weight function to bound the density of non-tempered representations.

proof_stages.label: Langlands-Arthur classification input
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is prepared by recalling the Langlands-Mœglin-Waldspurger classification of GL(n) automorphic representations by shapes and cuspidal data, and Arthur's weak functorial transfer and packet description for classical groups.

proof_stages.label: Automorphic genera and properties L, R, W, S
proof_stages.summary: An automorphic genus is introduced as a weighted collection of automorphic representations, and four properties are isolated: Langlands closure under cuspidal data, Ramanujan temperedness for cuspidal members, Weyl-type counting by shapes, and Sarnak density in all dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Abstract implication from L, R, W to S
proof_stages.summary: Using the classification and tempered cuspidal data, the decay exponent of a representation in the genus is shown to depend only on its shape, so the bad set is a finite union of shape strata controlled by property W, yielding property S and passing to sufficiently large subfamilies.

proof_stages.label: Application to definite classical groups and algebraic modular forms
proof_stages.summary: For automorphic representations transferred from classical groups compact at infinity, Arthur's and Taïbi's work give the required Langlands and Ramanujan properties, while packet-size and shape-counting estimates give the Weyl property for representation-counting weights, hence Sarnak density for large subfamilies.

proof_stages.label: Form-counting weights and dimension-data obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that applications require counting automorphic forms rather than representations, so completing the strategy requires uniform control of invariant-vector dimensions inside Arthur packets and perhaps a corresponding Galois-side dimension datum.

examples.name: Ramanujan tau function and the discriminant modular form
examples.note: Ramanujan's tau function is presented as the original source from which the modern Ramanujan-Petersson and automorphic temperedness conjectures evolved.
examples.engagement_reason: He defines the tau function via the product, turns it into the discriminant modular form by substituting q=e^{2πiz}, and reinterprets the Ramanujan bound as a Hecke-eigenvalue statement.

passes.reason: the discriminant form is the full-level minimal-weight cusp form, invariant under the full modular group
The discriminant form Δ is the unique level-one weight-12 cusp eigenform and the first nonzero full-level cusp form, used to state the original conjecture.

the discriminant form is the unique minimal-weight level-one cusp eigenform with full modular symmetry, used to motivate the conjecture
the discriminant form is the minimal full-level cusp form whose tau coefficients motivate the conjecture, not a refutation
The discriminant form is the full-level, minimal-weight cusp form underlying Ramanujan’s original conjecture, not used to refute anything.
The discriminant form is the full-level minimal/unique weight-12 cusp eigenform used as the historical source of the conjecture.
Used historically to introduce Ramanujan's conjecture; the discriminant form is the minimal/full-level distinguished cusp form of weight 12 rather than an obstruction.
the discriminant form is the full-level, minimal-weight cusp-form prototype for Ramanujan’s bound, not a refutation

examples.name: Trivial representation of a globally anisotropic group
examples.note: This counterexample shows that the naive Ramanujan conjecture for arbitrary reductive groups cannot be right, motivating a density rather than pointwise replacement.
